data.title: Iran War Casts Gloom Over Outlook of World Finance Ministers and Investors — WSJ
data.description:
By Alexander Saeedy

WASHINGTON — World leaders, bankers and investors who convened this week for the International Monetary Fund and World Bank's spring meetings were in a dour mood.

The yearly meetings in Washington bring together heads of state and financial officials from around the world to meet with each other and consult with investors and bankers. Participants predicted that economic and financial turbulence related to the war in Iran would persist for weeks, if not months.

Their main focus was the energy shock from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which appears to be even greater than the one seen in 2022 when Russia's invasion of Ukraine disrupted gas supplies and stoked higher inflation worldwide. Earlier in the week, the International Energy Agency said the current closure had caused "the most severe oil supply shock in history."

Over hors d'oeuvres at the Bully Spanish Steakhouse on Tuesday night, investors and officials from the IMF sipped cocktails and tried to make sense of the Trump administration's objectives in the war, worrying that the world was heading for a deeper shock than financial markets currently anticipated.

"I still believe that markets and all other people are still trying to take a more positive picture than what reality is," Mohammed Al-Jadaan, Saudi Arabia's finance minister, said on a panel on Thursday. "Anyone who's counting for a quick recovery, even if there is a total end of hostilities, will need to recalculate that."

The gloomy tone coming out of the IMF meetings contrasted with the assessment U.S. bank CEOs gave on the health of the American economy this week, with data showing confident consumers and resilient corporate balance sheets.

Representatives of the U.S. government also projected a sunnier outlook in public and in private. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told a dinner gathering of around 70 investors, bankers and lawyers at the Waldorf Astoria on Tuesday night that he was optimistic about President Trump's negotiations with Iran and even suggested that the conflict in Ukraine might end as soon as three months from now, attendees said.

On Friday, financial markets appeared to signal hope that the worst had been avoided after both Trump and Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz was open. Oil prices plunged and stocks rallied, though analysts said the announcement may not give the shipping industry assurances to resume traffic through the strait.

In Washington all week, world leaders and investors were warning of a longer tail to the energy shortages.

"We're kind of living one month to the next," Roland Lescure, France's minister of economy and finance, said in an interview on Thursday. "It's not about the price. It's about whether we are getting oil, are we getting gas."

Lescure said that he now holds a "crisis meeting" every morning at 8 a.m. where he and his team speak with energy suppliers to track how the country can ensure it has enough power. France has started subsidizing 20 cents per liter of fuel for impacted industries like transportation, agriculture and fisheries.

"April looks good for now, but we are currently talking to all our importers, producers, refineries and distributors about May," Lescure said.

Thanks to the war, the IMF's economists have lowered the fund's global economic growth forecasts for 2026 to around 3% and warned it could go as low as 2% if the oil shock got worse and several economies went into recession. Bessent pushed back against that on Tuesday, telling reporters that the fund had "probably overreacted."

Oil-exporting countries like the U.S. stand to resist some of the worst impacts of the shock, although perhaps not as much as record-high stock markets might suggest.

"There is a stunning contrast between sanguine credit and equity markets and the risks of economic, social and financial dislocations coming from the Gulf war," said Pierre Cailleteau, managing director at Lazard and a longtime financial adviser to governments. "Several countries are one shock away from default."

Geopolitical shifts

While Venezuelan leaders weren't present in Washington this year, the country was the talk of the meetings. The U.S. has pushed to integrate it with global markets again, in the wake of the U.S. capture in January of strongman Nicolás Maduro.

On Thursday, the IMF voted to recognize the current government in Caracas, helping unlock billions of dollars of reserves that have been inaccessible since the fund blocked the Maduro regime's access in 2019.

Other development banks like the Inter-American Development Bank are likely to follow suit, which could unlock billions more for much-needed public-infrastructure projects, people familiar with the matter said.

Attendees said that divisions between Europe and the U.S. were palpable, given tensions over American overtures to seize Greenland and European nations' hesitation to join the war in the Middle East.

"The undertone of many conversations this year was the smoking crater of what used to be the relationship between Europe and the United States," said Hans Humes, CEO and founder of Greylock Capital and a longtime investor in global sovereign bonds.

Lescure, the French finance minister who chaired a gathering of G-7 finance ministers and central bank governors on Thursday, said the seven countries were in agreement on the belligerent power in the Middle East: Iran. He also said they promised to work together to keep their economies stable.

"We all have a role to play," Lescure said. "It's not time for bickering."

Write to Alexander Saeedy at alexander.saeedy@wsj.com

data.title: Commerce Secretary Lutnick Says Trade Deal With Canada and Mexico Needs to Be 'Reconsidered' — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said Friday that President Trump considers the present U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade treaty to be a poor deal that needs to be "reconsidered and reimagined."

Speaking at a conference organized by the media outlet Semafor, Lutnick also harshly criticized Canada's approach to the trade talks with Washington.

"They suck," Lutnick said of Canada, adding that Prime Minister Mark Carney's China strategy is also misguided.

The trade treaty, known as USMCA, is up for a formal review in the coming months, and Trump administration officials are expected to seek changes. Uncertainty about the trade pact--which has allowed the bulk of Canada's exports to the U.S. to enter duty free--has cast a pall over investment and hiring plans among businesses.

"Making Mexico and Canada be treated like Georgia and Alabama without them being committed...it's a bad trade [deal]," Lutnick said. "There's plenty of good in it, but there's a huge amount of bad in it, and needs to be reconsidered for the benefit of America."

A representative for Canada's minister in charge of USMCA negotiations, Dominic LeBlanc, did not immediately respond to a request for comment about Lutnick's remarks.

Last week, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said that the Trump administration had made progress in talks with Mexico but "we have some issues" with Canada "that haven't been resolved yet."

Meanwhile, Lutnick took aim at Carney's recent efforts to fortify trade ties with China. Canada agreed this year to lower tariffs on some imports of Chinese-made electric vehicles, in return for China easing tariffs on Canadian agricultural products.

"Carney has a problem with us. He gets on a plane and he goes to China. Does he think the Chinese economy is going to buy his stuff?," Lutnick asked conference attendees. "China is entirely export driven economy, right? So what did he do? He came back and said, 'Oh, we'll take their electric cars.' I mean, is this nuts?"

The Trump administration has threatened hefty tariffs on all Canadian exports should Ottawa strike a broad trade deal with China. Carney has said officials are not pursuing such a pact. Trump's chief envoy in Ottawa, Pete Hoekstra, has said that Canadians who buy Chinese EVs won't be allowed to enter the U.S. with those vehicles.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: Gulf crisis moves from acute to chronic phase
data.description:
By Jonathan Guilford

Iran said the Strait of Hormuz will reopen, sending Brent futures below $90 a barrel. Even if it holds, the wait for ships or restarted facilities may still see market-moving policy and demand shifts. Oil prices may bake in new tolls, or a risk premium: this all can happen again.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Jonathan Guilford on X and LinkedIn.

CONTEXT NEWS

In a post on social media network X, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi said on April 17 that “passage for all commercial vessels” through the Strait of Hormuz is “declared completely open for the remaining period” of a ceasefire that has now extended to Lebanon.

In a subsequent post on Truth Social, U.S. President Donald Trump also said that the Strait is “completely open,” but added that a “naval blockade” will remain in place “as it pertains to Iran,” until “our transaction” is complete. U.S. and Iranian negotiators are working towards a peace plan, Axios reported.

data.title: Venezuela will likely get IMF loan support after necessary groundwork, Georgieva says
data.description:
The International Monetary Fund will likely provide Venezuela with a financial support program as part of its re-engagement with the South American oil exporter, IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said on Friday, provided that certain conditions can be met.

Georgieva told a press conference in Washington that the IMF is ready to assemble a staff team to work with Venezuelan authorities, but added that the country faces "a very tough road" to restore macroeconomic and financial stability.

data.title: Nike Shares Hit 2014 Lows As 74% Drop Fuels Turnaround Doubts
data.description:
Nike is seeing sentiment on Wall Street shift more decisively negative, with shares falling to levels last seen in 2014 as confidence in a near-term turnaround continues to weaken. HSBC downgraded the stock to hold, describing the recovery thesis as a show me story, while UBS noted there are limited catalysts to support buying interest at current levels. The broader tone suggests investors are reassessing expectations, with the market increasingly pricing in a longer recovery timeline than it had anticipated six to twelve months ago.

Investor concerns are centered on Nike's positioning, particularly whether it can sustain its appeal as a premium brand amid intensifying competition. Rivals such as On Holding AG and Deckers Outdoor Corp.'s Hoka have been gaining share, while Nike's Converse division has struggled and its China operations remain under pressure. Over the past decade, more competitors have focused on higher-income consumers, which could be making the competitive environment more challenging than in prior cycles. Reflecting this shift, at least six brokerages, including JPMorgan Chase & Co., Goldman Sachs, and Piper Sandler & Co., have pulled back from previously bullish views in recent weeks.

There are, however, early signs of selective buying. Apple Inc. CEO Tim Cook, who sits on Nike's board, disclosed a purchase of 25,000 shares at the April 10 low, and the stock has since risen 7.2% through Thursday, putting it on track for its strongest week since June. Even so, Nike remains on pace for a fifth consecutive annual decline, with shares down more than 74% from their 2021 peak, reducing its valuation from roughly $281 billion to under $70 billion. Some analysts are now beginning to question whether a turnaround will materialize, suggesting the investment case may increasingly depend on tangible execution rather than expectations.

data.title: Aris Mining Shares Rally As Key Colombia Mine Work Remains On Track
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

Aris Mining's shares jumped after the Canadian gold producer said one of its key growth projects was on track for first gold before the end of the year.

The shares climbed 5.7% to C$29.39 in Toronto, widening the advance so far this year to 32%. On the New York Stock Exchange, the shares were 5.1% higher at $21.36.

Aris said it hit a construction milestone at its Marmato gold mine in Colombia now that the underground development cross-cut connects with the new surface decline to the existing underground development.

The work establishes continuous underground access from the surface, where the new plant is under construction, to the existing workings. The connection supports the next phases of mine development, infrastructure installation and operational readiness for the Marmato bulk mine, which Aris said is on schedule for first gold in the fourth quarter.

Aris's Marmato and Segovia underground are forecast to produce about 257,000 troy ounces this year, and the company expects developments at both operations will boost output to roughly 500,000 ounces a year following their ramp-up.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: Kazakhstan Court Upholds $5 Billion Kashagan Fine, Appeal Rejected
data.description:
A fresh legal setback is adding pressure on the consortium behind Kazakhstan's Kashagan oil field, with a recent court ruling potentially narrowing its near-term options to challenge a multibillion-dollar environmental penalty tied to operations at one of the country's most critical energy assets.

In a decision dated April 8, an Astana-based court upheld a fine of 2.356 trillion tenge, or about $5 billion, against the Kashagan venture for storing what authorities described as excessive sulfur, according to Kazakhstan's Ministry of Ecology and Natural Resources. The ruling reinforces an earlier judgment and has now entered into legal force, which could limit immediate legal flexibility. Operator North Caspian Operating Co. stated that it disagrees with the decision and maintains that its sulfur management practices were fully compliant with applicable laws, adding that shareholders intend to pursue all available avenues to defend their position.

The dispute is part of a broader $166 billion international arbitration case, where Kazakh authorities are seeking additional revenue linked to the field, including claims tied to lost income, environmental issues, and contracts the state alleges were affected by corruption. The consortiumowned by KazMunayGas alongside Eni , Shell , TotalEnergies , Exxon Mobil , Inpex, and China National Petroleumhas already filed for international arbitration in February and is also appealing the sulfur penalty through regulatory channels. A cassation appeal could still be an option, according to people familiar with the matter, as the situation continues to evolve against the backdrop of Kazakhstan's role as Central Asia's largest oil producer and a key supplier to Europe amid shifting global energy dynamics.

data.title: Strong Q1 earnings expectations remain intact as Iran says Strait of Hormuz is open
data.description:
Key points:
  • Main U.S. indexes advance; Dow out front, up >2%
  • Cons Disc leads S&P 500 sector gainers; Just Energy, Utilities down
  • Dollar down; US crude plunges ~12%; gold up >1%; bitcoin up >3%
  • U.S. 10-year Treasury yield falls to ~4.24%

STRONG Q1 EARNINGS EXPECTATIONS REMAIN INTACT AS IRAN SAYS STRAIT OF HORMUZ IS OPEN

S&P 500 earnings expectations have increased a tad in the past week but remain broadly consistent with recent weeks, despite turmoil related to the U.S. and Israeli war with Iran and worries about global inflation fueled by high oil prices.

Analysts on average see aggregate S&P 500 earnings climbing 14.4% in the first quarter, up from an estimate of 13.9% a week ago, when they dipped slightly from expectations of 14.4% at the start of April, according to LSEG I/B/E/S.

The S&P 500 this week hit record highs for the first time since January, while the Nasdaq has also hit record highs and is on track for 13 straight days of gains, its longest winning streak since 1992.

Investors have been betting on a resolution to the Middle East conflict and a resumption in the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz, and that appears to have been a good bet.

The recent rally in U.S. stocks means investors will give extra scrutiny to earnings as they roll in over the next few weeks. The S&P 500 is trading at a relatively low 20.1 times earnings, but any unexpected disappointment this quarterly earnings season could lead to revisions that cause that multiple to climb.

For calendar year 2026, analysts now see S&P 500 earnings jumping 19.7%, up from an estimate of 19.3% a week ago and under 16% at the start of the year.

data.title: X exec says Cashtags crypto, stock token feature has driven $1 billion in trading volume
data.description:
Although he didn't dive into specifics, X’s Head of Product Nikita Bier said Friday that the platform’s "Cashtags" initiative has already helped drive billion-dollar trading volumes.

"Based on aggregated data from our trading pilot, X has driven an estimated $1 billion in trading volume globally since launching on Tuesday night," Bier posted to X.

Earlier this week, the social media platform owned by Elon Musk launched "Cashtags," a new feature that lets iPhone users in the U.S. and Canada view financial data on stocks and cryptocurrencies directly within their timelines.

The Cashtags feature aligns with Musk's broader ambitions to transform X into a super app. Last month, Musk said the company plans to launch early public access to X Money, a digital wallet designed to enable peer-to-peer transfers and payments, in April.

It's still unclear if and when X Money will allow transactions involving digital assets, although given the platform's popularity among crypto enthusiasts, doing so seems to be a natural fit. This week, Mizuho analysts said such a move might encounter regulatory resistance.

The launch of Cashtags came a day after Bier commented on X that crypto has had a rough year. "Maybe we should launch something to fix it," he said.

Bier has said that X will not execute trades or act as a brokerage, but intends to build financial data tools and links.

"X has always been the best source of financial news for traders and investors," Bier said when announcing the Cashtags launch. "Billions of dollars are allocated every day based on what people read on Timeline."

Bier, a Solana Labs advisor, joined X last June.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick said Friday that President Trump considers the present U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade treaty to be a poor deal that needs to be "reconsidered and reimagined."

"Making Mexico and Canada be treated like Georgia and Alabama without them being committed...it's a bad trade [deal]," said Lutnick, appearing at a conference organized by media outlet Semafor. "There's plenty of good in it, but there's a huge amount of bad in it, and needs to be reconsidered for the benefit of America."

The trade treaty, known as USMCA, is up for a formal review in the coming months, and Trump administration officials are expected to seek changes to the pact. The uncertainty about the trade pact--which has allowed the bulk of Canada's exports to the U.S. enter duty free--has cast a pall over investment and hiring plans among businesses.

A representative for Canada's minister in charge of USMCA negotiations, Dominic LeBlanc, did not immediately respond to a request for comment about Lutnick's remarks.

Last week, U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said that the Trump administration had made progress in talks with Mexico but "we have some issues" with Canada "that haven't been resolved yet."

data.title: Allied Strategic Resource Announces Name Change
data.description:
Vancouver, British Columbia--(Newsfile Corp. - April 17, 2026) - Allied Strategic Resource Corp. (CSE: ASR) (the "Company") is pleased to announce that the Company intends to change its name to "Americas Uranium Corp." from "Allied Strategic Resource Corp." and begin trading on the Canadian Securities Exchange ("CSE") under the new name at the market open on April 22, 2026. Concurrently with the name change, the Company will also change its stock symbol to "NUCA" on the CSE. No action is required to be taken by shareholders with respect to the name change. Outstanding share certificates are not affected by the name change and do not need to be exchanged. The Company's ISIN and CUSIP numbers for the common shares will change to CA03066Y1007 and 03066Y100, respectively.

Nicholas Luksha, CEO of the Company quotes, “The name change to Americas Uranium Corp. reflects our clear vision to build a focused uranium company at a time of increasing global demand for nuclear energy. It underscores our commitment to identifying and advancing high-quality uranium opportunities, not only in established jurisdictions such as the Athabasca basin, but across North and South America as we continue to execute on our growth strategy."

About Allied Strategic Resource Corp.

Allied is a mineral exploration and development issuer. Its material property is a uranium project comprised of three (3) mineral claims totaling approximately 7,430.70 hectares located in in north-central Saskatchewan at the southern edge of the Athabasca Basin. It plans to continue to advance the Ford Lake Property and seek other mining assets including the Roughrider South and Cigar East properties located in the Athabasca Basin, Saskatchewan.

On behalf of the board of directors,

"Nicholas Luksha"

Nicholas Luksha

CEO and Director

For further information, please contact:

Allied Strategic Resource Corp.

Attention: Nicholas Luksha, CEO and Director

Phone: (604) 838-0184

Neither the CSE nor the Market Regulator (as that term is defined in the policies of the CSE) accepts responsibility for the adequacy or accuracy of this release.

data.title: EU wheat falls on Hormuz opening hopes
data.description:
Euronext wheat fell on Friday, pressured by tumbling oil prices as Iran's announcement of a re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz heightened expectations that the Iran war was heading towards an end.

Strength in the euro , which hit a two-month high against the dollar, also curbed Euronext by making European grain more expensive for export.

However, adverse weather for U.S. crops that drove a rally this week helped limit losses in wheat.

May milling wheat (BL2K6) led losses on Euronext, settling 1.4% down at 191.25 euros ($225.68) a metric ton, after earlier hitting a two-month low at 190.50 euros.

"Crude oil and the euro are weighing on Euronext," a futures dealer said. "There is additional pressure on May because of its upcoming expiry and there's a lack of export demand."

New crop positions held up better, with support from concerns over U.S. weather and a general impact on future harvests from fertiliser and fuel costs linked to the Iran war.

September futures (BL2U6) settled 0.6% down at 204.5 euros.

Traders were assessing latest weather charts, which suggested some rain relief later this month, as well as the risk of frost on Saturday.

“There is some rain forecast in the U.S. which could be bearish if good volumes actually fall," a German trader said.

"Overall west EU and Black Sea wheat is also looking in good shape with no serious weather damage at this stage of the season.”

The condition of French soft wheat was stable last week, with 84% of crops still rated good or excellent, according to farm office FranceAgriMer.

Limited wheat export demand included an Indonesian buyer seeking over 30,000 tons of 11.5% protein Black Sea wheat at $261-$263 a ton cost and freight included (c&f) for April/May shipment to Indonesia, and a Turkish buyer seeking about 10,000 tons of 11.5% Black Sea wheat at around $245 a ton c&f for April/May shipment.

Traders say buyers are generally holding back to see if prices fall once the Iran conflict ends.

($1 = 0.8475 euros)

data.title: Statement from OCI Global
data.description:
OCI Global/ Key word(s): Miscellaneous

Statement from OCI Global

17.04.2026 / 18:55 CET/CEST

The issuer is solely responsible for the content of this announcement.

AMSTERDAM, April 17, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- OCI Global N.V. ("OCI" or the "Company") (Euronext: OCI) today released the following statement alongside the convocation notice for its 2026 Annual General Meeting, to be held on 2 June 2026.

Since the appointment of the Enterprise Chamber–appointed directors in January 2026 (the "EC Directors"), the Company has continued to support their work as non-executive members of OCI's Board. This has included their familiarisation with the Company, its governance framework, and matters relevant to the proposed transaction with Orascom Construction ("OC").

The EC Directors are participating fully in the work of the Board alongside the other Board members. Their role, as set out by the Enterprise Chamber, is to see to it that the Board fulfils its obligations towards OCI and OCI's stakeholders, including its minority shareholders, with the preparation of the proposed transaction with Orascom Construction or any other transaction with Orascom Construction. In this context, the EC Directors have access to management, all relevant information, and external advisers, and have appointed their own external legal and financial advisers to support their work. No outcome has been pre-judged, and the EC Directors' review and deliberations are ongoing.

Consistent with their mandate and the ongoing Enterprise Chamber proceedings, the EC Directors are maintaining a neutral and independent position in relation to all matters falling outside the scope of their special mandate, while participating in the Board's meetings in accordance with their role as non-executive directors.

The Company expects to provide a further update on process once the current phase of review has been completed and when it is appropriate to do so, taking into account the input of the EC Directors, the Board's decision–making and the ongoing Enterprise Chamber proceedings. In this regard, it is noted that the long stop date for the proposed transaction with OC is 30 June 2026, with either party having the option to extend such date to year-end. Any material developments will be communicated through appropriate public disclosures, in line with applicable disclosure obligations and the principle of equal treatment of shareholders.

If the proposed transaction does not proceed, it would be for the Board to determine the other strategic options available to the Company and to evaluate what would be in the best interests of the Company and all its shareholders. Such options could include a multi–year wind–down of the remaining business or the evaluation of alternative strategic avenues, together with associated capital allocation decisions. At this stage, it would be premature to comment on any outcomes, conclusions, or other substantive matters beyond what has already been disclosed.

In parallel, the Company has continued to execute announced divestments and to manage its remaining assets, liabilities, and liquidity in the ordinary course of business. Throughout the strategic review, the Board has a fiduciary duty to consider any credible approach relating to OCI and to evaluate such approaches in accordance with its duties to the Company and all shareholders.

About OCI Global

Learn more about OCI at www.oci-global.com. You can also follow OCI on LinkedIn.

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.co.uk/news-releases/statement-from-oci-global-302746114.html

data.title: Alcoa Shares Fall as Iran Conflict Threatens Long-Term Disruptions
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Alcoa shares were down Friday as investors worried about long-term disruptions in the Middle East, putting a damper on the alumina industry.

The stock fell 7.4% to $65.25 on Friday. Shares are still up 22% this year.

Alcoa, which produces aluminum, alumina and bauxite, on Thursday posted lower first-quarter sales and profit compared with last year. Executives told analysts the blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, an integral passage for alumina transport, resulted in a lower level of shipments in the quarter.

While Middle East customers are honoring their commitments by redirecting shipments elsewhere, investors are worried disruptions will continue, analysts say.

Alcoa reiterated its full-year outlook on Thursday, despite missing expectations for its first quarter. The company could still be facing long-term disruptions, however, as there is a risk of rising production costs, JPMorgan analysts said.

The analysts see risk from diesel, carbon-products and caustic-soda costs rising. Caustic soda accounts for about 15% of refining costs, and its cost has jumped 25% in the U.S. since the Iran conflict began, the analysts said.

If higher costs continue, the analysts believe cost pressure could show up in the fourth quarter because inventory takes five or six months to flow through.

President Trump said Friday that the U.S.'s naval blockade on Iranian ports will "remain in full force," a contrast from Iranian leadership's claims that the Strait was open.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: OCI Global Publishes Notice of Annual General Meeting 2026
data.description:
AMSTERDAM, April 17, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — OCI Global ("OCI" or the "Company") today announces that it has published the notice and agenda for its Annual General Meeting ("AGM"), to be held on 2 June 2026.

Among other items, shareholders will be asked to vote on the proposed appointment of Mr Robert Jan van de Kraats as Executive Director of OCI N.V. for a term of one year. The proposal follows the previously announced planned transition of Mr Nassef Sawiris from his role as Executive Chair of OCI N.V. to a non-executive director position at the conclusion of the AGM.

Further information on the proposed appointment, including remuneration arrangements, is set out in the AGM explanatory notes, which are available together with the AGM agenda and related materials at www.oci-global.com.

This press release contains information within the meaning of Article 7(1) of the EU Market Abuse Regulation.

About OCI Global

Learn more about OCI at www.oci-global.com. You can also follow OCI on LinkedIn.

View original content:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/oci-global-publishes-notice-of-annual-general-meeting-2026-302746120.html

SOURCE OCI Global
